Mini Source

This repository is a fixture for glance-core tests, built around a deliberately tiny Rust parsing path and one documentation area whose only explicit rule is that it must stay out of parser-only regeneration plans.

What this is

At the top level, the repository identifies itself only as Mini Source and says its purpose is to act as a fixture repository for glance-core tests.

The executable core of that fixture is a tiny Rust library that exposes a parser module and one public function, answer, which returns a u32 by calling the parser with the literal text "42".

Alongside that code path is a documentation directory whose guide defines one hard rule: the directory should not be included in a parser-only regeneration plan.

Role in the whole

As a test fixture, the repository’s whole job is to provide glance-core with a small, stable source tree whose meaning can be recovered from very little code and one sharply stated documentation constraint.

The source side supplies a minimal end-to-end software path: a public library API delegates work to a child parser module and turns one fixed string into one typed numeric answer.

The documentation side supplies a contrasting kind of contract: not a parsing behavior, but a planning boundary, because the guide exists to state that parser-only regeneration must exclude that directory.

The design choice most likely to surprise a newcomer is how strict the parser path is: the parser does not model failure as data, and instead treats numeric input as a requirement by calling expect during conversion.

Composition

docs

A guarded documentation area whose guide exists to say this directory must not participate in parser-only regeneration planning.

src

The Rust library root that publishes the parser module and exposes the one public entry point, answer.

The repository’s working structure is therefore not broad inventory but one thin orchestrator at the source root and one child parser leaf that performs the actual string-to-number conversion.

At the bottom of that code path, the parser leaf contains a single function, parse_answer, which accepts &str and returns u32 by parsing directly.

Seams and contracts

The main code seam is the handoff from the library root to the parser child: the root passes a hard-coded string into the parser API, so the contract at that boundary is that the text must be suitable for numeric parsing.

That contract is load-bearing because the parser enforces it by parsing the input directly and calling expect with the message fixture input is numeric, making successful conversion the only supported outcome on this path.

A separate repository-level contract lives outside the Rust path: parser-only regeneration must not include the docs directory.

Together, those two contracts define the fixture’s shape: numeric text is mandatory on the code path, and exclusion of docs is mandatory on the parser-only planning path.

Where it can hurt you

The sharpest runtime edge is in the parser leaf, where non-numeric text becomes a panic rather than a recoverable result because conversion is wrapped in expect.

That edge propagates upward to the public API because answer depends completely on the fixed literal "42" remaining within the parser’s numeric contract.

The planning edge is different but equally explicit: if a parser-only regeneration plan accidentally includes docs, it violates the one rule stated by that directory’s guide.

Flows

  • Public answer flow, hop 1: the source root exposes the parser module and the public function answer, and that function starts the repository’s only top-level code path by supplying the literal text "42".
  • Public answer flow, hop 2: the parser leaf receives text through parse_answer, parses it directly into u32, and treats numeric input as required by using expect.
  • Module composition flow, hop 1: the library root binds the child parser into the visible API surface by declaring the module and delegating conversion work to it.
  • Module composition flow, hop 2: the child parser completes that delegated work by being the sole place where raw text is converted into the typed answer.
  • Regeneration-boundary flow, hop 1: the documentation guide states that the docs directory should not be included in a parser-only regeneration plan.
  • Regeneration-boundary flow, hop 2: in a repository whose stated purpose is to serve as a glance-core test fixture, that exclusion rule becomes part of the source material the generated site must preserve and communicate.

Data model

Stored shapes in the code path are minimal: the library root contains the literal string "42", publishes the parser module, and exposes a function signature that returns u32.

Stored shapes in the parser leaf are equally small: one function accepts an &str input and defines conversion into u32 as its only output shape.

Stored shapes in the documentation path reduce to one explicit rule in the guide: docs must be excluded from parser-only regeneration plans.

The primary derived shape is the typed numeric answer, produced when parser input text is successfully parsed into u32.

At the repository surface, the exported derived value is whatever answer gets back from the parser after handing off the fixed fixture string.

Failure-edge index

  • Non-numeric parser input is a hard failure edge: the parser uses expect, so invalid text causes a panic instead of producing a recoverable value.
  • The public answer path inherits that parser edge because it always routes through the same conversion function with the fixed text "42".
  • Parser-only regeneration has a separate planning failure edge: including docs violates the directory rule stated in the guide.
